content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To       : File                                                     Date  : Jan. 9, 1981


From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Sarah Cameron                                                A-81-01-001

Subject: Tickets to a school's own sporting events


       At the Conflicts of Interest seminar in Anaheim on January
6, 1981, someone from a school district asked whether the tickets
which the superintendent and board members get to the school's
athletic events were reportable gifts. Following the Commission's
determination in the case of the San Diego City Council's receipt
of boxes at San Diego Stadium, I advised that tickets to the
school's own sporting events were not gifts but were more akin to
benefits of office. I pointed out, however, that this only
applies because the district is giving the tickets to the officials,
that it does not apply to tickets received from private sporting
teams, etc.

sections.question_synthetic: Are tickets to a school's own athletic events that are provided to the superintendent and board members reportable gifts under California political practices law?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Tickets to a school's own sporting events are not reportable gifts but rather benefits of office, similar to the Commission's prior determination regarding San Diego City Council's receipt of boxes at San Diego Stadium. This determination applies only when the district itself provides the tickets to officials, and does not extend to tickets received from private sporting teams.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Informal memorandum format rather than standard formal advice letter. No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections present. Advice provided during seminar setting. Contains reference to prior Commission determination (San Diego City Council case) as precedent.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Are tickets to a school's own athletic events that are provided to the superintendent and board members reportable gifts under California political practices law?

CONCLUSION: Tickets to a school's own sporting events are not reportable gifts but rather benefits of office, similar to the Commission's prior determination regarding San Diego City Council's receipt of boxes at San Diego Stadium. This determination applies only when the district itself provides the tickets to officials, and does not extend to tickets received from private sporting teams.
embedding.first_500_words: State of California Memorandum To : File Date : Jan. 9, 1981 From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION Sarah Cameron A-81-01-001 Subject: Tickets to a school's own sporting events At the Conflicts of Interest seminar in Anaheim on January 6, 1981, someone from a school district asked whether the tickets which the superintendent and board members get to the school's athletic events were reportable gifts. Following the Commission's determination in the case of the San Diego City Council's receipt of boxes at San Diego Stadium, I advised that tickets to the school's own sporting events were not gifts but were more akin to benefits of office. I pointed out, however, that this only applies because the district is giving the tickets to the officials, that it does not apply to tickets received from private sporting teams, etc.
embedding.summary: FPPC memorandum providing informal advice at a Conflicts of Interest seminar regarding whether tickets to school athletic events constitute reportable gifts for school superintendents and board members.
